issue_title: Systems: A feature to change base units
issue_body:
See **Version 2** below

----

### Introduction

In Pint every unit is defined in terms of other units. We call these **derived units**. Notable exception are **base units** which are linked to dimensions (enclosed in square brackets `[]`). For example, in the [units definition file](https://github.com/hgrecco/pint/blob/master/pint/default_en.txt):

```
angstrom = 1e-10 * meter # derived unit
meter = [length] # base unit
```

This also creates the concept of **base dimensions**, i.e. the dimensions linked to base units (e.g. `[length]`, `[time]`) and **derived dimensions** (e.g. `[speed] = [length] / [time]`) 

Of course the system could be changed easily right now changing the definition file. 

Some possibilities are:

A redefinition of base units with the default base dimensions, for example:
```
meter = 1e10 * angstrom # derived unit
angstrom = [length] # base unit
```

A redefinition of base units with other base dimensions, for example:
```
hour = [time]  # base unit
mph = [speed] # base unit
mile = mph * hour  # derived unit
```

This creates a Directed Acyclic Graph relating each unit to one or more *basic* ones until base units are reached. In current version of Pint (0.6), base units are unprefixed SI units.

Additionally, all units in Pint are exposed to the user in the same way. There is no way to indicate that a certain unit belongs to certain family. For example, a user might want to obtain all compatible metric units but not imperial.

All together, base units, base dimensions and groups of units are what we call a **system**. 

### Goal

Being able to define and use a new system based on the definition file or programmatically as a change to an existing system.


### Defining a new system

Defining a new system involves substituting one or more base units with others.

```
@system <name> = <rule 1>, <rule 2>, ..., <rule N>
```

```python
>>> ureg.define_system(<name>, <rule 1>, <rule 2>, ..., <rule N>)
```

A rule is a string with two parts separated by a semicolon: `<new unit>:<old unit>`. The first part indicates a unit to be considered a base unit. The second is a unit not to be used as a base unit any more.

The `<old unit>` part is unnecessary if `<new unit>` has the same dimension of the base unit to be replaced. In other words: `inch` is interpreted as `inch:meter`.

A `ValueError` is raised if:
- two or more units with the same dimension are provided.
- the old unit part is not provided and new unit is not a base unit.
- the new unit and old unit are unrelated.

This syntax allows changing the system keeping or changing the same base dimensions.

### Using a named system

```
>>> ureg.set_system(<name>)
```

### Defining and using an unnamed system on the fly

```
>>> ureg.set_base_units(<rule 1>, <rule 2>, ..., <rule N>)
```

### Other changes to the API

The UnitRegistry constructor will accept an extra argument (`system`) to indicate the system to use. A string will be considered as the name of the system and a tuple or list will be considered as a list of rules. This will be executed after reading the files and before building the cache. It will default to a named system specified in the definition file with the following syntax.

```
@default system <name>
```

Some methods might be changed to accept and extra parameter specifying the system. 

### Implementation

After changing the base units:
1. the `_base_units_cache`  and `_dimensionality_cache` will be invalidated.
2. the `_units` dictionary will be updated with the new definitions.

### Development

We will create a temporary branch named `_systems` to land commits. Is a temporary branch, so commits might be reorganized before merging them into develop.

issue_title: user-canceling of KDE Wallet dialogs causes unhandled exception
issue_body:
Upon import of kwallet.backends (which is done upon import of kwallet), the KDE Wallet modules are imported and if the import succeeds the wallet is opened.

Opening the wallet will sometimes (if the wallet hasn't been opened in this session) request a password to unlock the wallet or (if the user hasn't granted perminant access to this application) ask if the application is allowed to access the wallet.

If the user declines either of these dialogs an exception will be raised because kwallet is None and a method call is attempted on it.

I've attached a fix for this problem, with tests.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/37
- Originally reported by: Benji York
- Originally created at: 2010-11-16T22:04:05.746

issue_title: Documentation: Move all API elements to top level
issue_body:
It's hard to figure out what classes and modules are available. Best workflow I have now is to read through code, grep, or use Google.

One idea is to put everything at the top level to simplify the API. The best approach I know of is to use __init__.py. Check out pip for an example: https://github.com/pypa/pip/blob/develop/pip/__init__.py

This would have two major benefits:
1. No more trying to figure out which file to include ("Of course IP is in Layer2, what did you expect? Layer3?").
2. Incidental documentation of what's in the API.
If a name ends with umlaut char such as 'ä', cleaning fails. To fix, re.search needs to be called with the re.UNICODE flag.
It would be nice to include some unit tests.  I'm not exactly sure how these tests would be performed or validate though.  I will have to think a lot more about how this would go down.

It would be much better if we had a way to lookup closest indexed color for any RGB color. This could be glued to terminal capabilities that are explored in other issues.

I found that string functions aren't consistently `None`-safe, i.e. `trunc`, `camel_case`, ... are `None`-safe but `explode` and others aren't.

I propose string functions to be `None`-safe as is in [Apache Commons Lang](http://commons.apache.org/proper/commons-lang/javadocs/api-release/index.html) and in [underscore.string](https://github.com/epeli/underscore.string).

I also propose: `_.to_string(None)` returns `''` instead of `'None'` actually.

issue_title: How to use array indexing notation with traditional where query form?
issue_body:
In the Queries section of the Advanced Usage page of the docs, the example of a nested field query is given.

> db.search(User.birthday.year == 1990)

This can also be expressed using array indexing notation

> db.search(User['birthday']['year'] == 1990)

But how can array indexing notation be combined with the *where()* shorthand query form?

didn't work as expected, I tried the following variations, with no luck.

> db.search(where('birthday','year') == 1990)
> db.search(where('birthday'.'year') == 1990)
> db.search(where(['birthday'].['year']) == 1990)
> db.search(where(['birthday']).['year'] == 1990)
> db.search(where(['birthday')['year'] == 1990)
> db.search(where(['birthday']).'year' == 1990)

Am I missing something, or isn't this supported?

issue_title: IPv6 Reserved Network Constants may be wrong.
issue_body:
The set of IPv6 networks listed as reserved networks in the constants of ipaddress.py seem incorrect.

For instance, when I look at a random IPv6 I generated (caec:cec6:c4ef:bb7b:1a78:d055:216d:3a78), the result of

The issue of acceleration came up in #68 , either by numba or by cython.  It seems like we should branch the discussion off into its own issue.

### Cython

* Pro: minimal dependencies, pip installable, wheels
* Con: packaging can be tricky, significant developer time required

### Numba

* Pro: minimal developer time required
* Con: not pip installable due to LLVM dependency


My two cents: despite the dependency chain problem, I'd prefer numba over cython because it would be easier to deploy across the entire module.

In librosa, we handled this by making the numba dependency optional, and providing a dummy decorator `optional_jit` that does a no-op if numba is not available, and jit-compilation if it is.  That way, everything still works, but if you want to get the acceleration benefits, it's on you to install numba.

Side note: if we also add conda(-forge) package distribution, we can easily add numba as a hard dependency there.

What do y'all think?

I'm running into validation failures in some segment data due to [this check](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/util.py#L708-L710).  I can't remember why we require non-empty intervals, but I don't think there's a technical reason for it in terms of div-by-zero errors in any of the metrics.  I suspect this came out of some of the weirder conversion errors in parsing the salami dataset.

Could we relax this check to instead fail only if `intervals[:, 1] < intervals[:, 0]` (negative duration) and throw a warning if they're equal?

I think `Environment` could be changed to not take the `prefix` by default, but get it then together with the version then:

```diff

 1 file changed, 16 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)

-            process = GeneralizedPopen([self.executable, '--version'], stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE)
+            process = GeneralizedPopen([
+                self.executable,
+                'import sys; print(sys.prefix); '
+                'print("%d.%d.%d" % sys.version_info[:3])'],
+                stdout=PIPE, stderr=PIPE)
             stdout, stderr = process.communicate()
             retcode = process.poll()
             if retcode:
@@ -81,12 +85,16 @@ def _get_version(self):
         except OSError:
             raise InvalidPythonEnvironment()

+            raise InvalidPythonEnvironment(
+                "Could not get version information (stdout=%r, stderr=%r)" % (
+                    stdout, stderr))
+        match_version = re.match(r'(\d+)\.(\d+)\.(\d+)', output[1])
+        if match_version is None:
+            raise InvalidPythonEnvironment(
+                "Could not get version information (stdout=%r, stderr=%r)" % (
+                    stdout, stderr))
+
+        # TODO: save sys.prefix - does it work with the Windows registry handling?
 
         return _VersionInfo(*[int(m) for m in match.groups()])
 
```
Downside: `python --version` is slightly faster than `python -c 'print(__import__("sys").version_info)'`
Ref: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/29980673/is-the-output-of-python-v-considered-to-be-stable-usable-as-an-api

However, this could also be made to get sys.path with the same call, and then you have a single call instead of potentially three.

issue_title: PartialEmoji instances are not equatable to Emoji instances
issue_body:
Currently, `PartialEmoji` is a `namedtuple` subclass and `Emoji` is a class. However, they are not able to be `==`ed.

Because `PartialEmoji` is a `namedtuple`, comparison logic follows tuple comparison logic. However, this makes it fundamentally incompatible with `Emoji`, which is a class and uses the `Hashable` mixin (and `EqualityComparable` by extension).

I think that these two types should be able to be equatable (`__eq__`), because they both represent the same concept (an emoji) and are interchangeable in return values by the library.

## Motivation/pitfalls

The reaction methods in the library accept some form of `Union[Emoji, PartialEmoji]`:

- `Message.remove_reaction`
- `Message.add_reaction`

They can also potentially be present in `Message.reactions` and are always present in `RawReactionActionEvent`.

### Comparing reactions

Comparing the `PartialEmoji` used in a reaction with an `Emoji` does not work as one would expect:

```py
some_emoji = bot.get_emoji(...)
reaction.emoji == some_emoji  # always evaluates to False
```

Alternatively, the `emoji` field in `RawReactionActionEvent`s:

```py
async def on_raw_reaction_add(self, event):
    emoji = event.emoji
    if emoji == self.emojis.green_tick_emoji:  # always evaluates to False
        approve(event)
    else:
        deny(event)
```

I feel that this is heavily misleading and could easily be a source of problems.

## Rudimentary implementation

```diff
diff --git a/discord/emoji.py b/discord/emoji.py
index 21c11c7..93f7268 100644
--- a/discord/emoji.py
+++ b/discord/emoji.py
@@ -98,6 +98,11 @@ class PartialEmoji(namedtuple('PartialEmoji', 'animated name id')):
         _format = 'gif' if self.animated else 'png'
         return "https://cdn.discordapp.com/emojis/{0.id}.{1}".format(self, _format)
 
+    def __eq__(self, other):
+        if isinstance(other, (Emoji, PartialEmoji)):
+            return other.id == self.id
+        return False
+
 class Emoji(Hashable):
     """Represents a custom emoji.
 
@@ -175,6 +180,12 @@ class Emoji(Hashable):
     def __repr__(self):
         return '<Emoji id={0.id} name={0.name!r}>'.format(self)
 
+    def __eq__(self, other):
+        if isinstance(other, PartialEmoji):
+            return other.id == self.id
+        else:
+            return Hashable.__eq__(self, other)
+
     @property
     def created_at(self):
         """Returns the emoji's creation time in UTC."""
```

issue_title: Server API response seems to have changed, successful file upload raises KeyError
issue_body:
I noticed my watcher went crazy last night because of an unexpected response from the server, which caused it to go into a loop and upload the same torrent over and over. This could have resulted in a massive amount of bandwidth used if the files had been downloaded again as well, so the bug seems pretty serious to me.

The thing that triggered the error was the post-request status check:

    if response['status'] == 'ERROR':
        ...

If there is no error, the key status does not exist, which raises a KeyError: 'status'.

In the case of a file upload, which is what I was doing, the response was:

    {u'transfer': { u'status': 'IN_QUEUE', ... }}

There is a status key inside transfer, but the top-level one is missing.

This only started happening last night. Server change?

issue_body:
Here's a minimal example:

```python

parser = configargparse.getArgumentParser()
parser.add('--foo', env_var='MY_FOO')
subparsers = parser.add_subparsers(help='Choose command')
sub_parser = subparsers.add_parser('sub')

issue_title: Move to a Trie to match tlds
issue_body:
Hello @barseghyanartur.

It is possible to enhance the performance and maybe memory footprint of your library (although I am not completely sure since needing more objects might be more RAM costly than just having a ~10k items hashset) by storing the tlds in a Trie in reverse order. This would mean more straightforward queries (just need to query the Trie to find the tld + more efficient misses if the query is not a tld etc.). I am not completely sure it would be more efficient though because it may mean more set/dict lookups than the current version but it depends on the average number of stems in the domain string.

What do you think? I can draft a PR to implement this if you feel this is a good way to go.

issue_title: Extension decorator should return the class
issue_body:
Using the `@library.extension` decorator (in master, it doesn't work in 1.4.1 as per #153) results in the class being set to `None` in the module where it is defined, which isn't a massive deal since it does get registered – unless you happen to reference the class in the file (or anywhere else) e.g. in a `super` call.

I expect the decorator needs to just return the class unmodified since all it's doing is appending it to the array of extensions.

I've found that some antiviruses recognize this javascript as "trojan script", but
it's already confirmed as false positive. One of report is:

https://www.virustotal.com/#/file/86db91d4ec31f22f619b887863877f16394f2e88f6b5e426849e569cd4a8df0d/detection

This script is included in some WP plugins and it's confusion for some users (and it's trouble with automatic scanners on some web hosting). Also, it's too boring to explain it again and again.

By my quick research, it seems that this line is "source":
```js
return new Blob([String.fromCharCode(0xFEFF), blob], {type: blob.type});
```
Simple replacement of `String.fromCharCode(0xFEFF)` with `"\uFEFF"` resolves this issue. Other code which passes is `String.fromCharCode(0xFEFF) + blob` (but I don't know is it good solution).

I'm not JS expert and I know that's better to use `String.fromCharCode(0xFEFF)`. Please help us.

Regards,
Sasa

# Opening XlsxWriter Issues

Title: Issue with constant_memory

Hi,

I am using XlsxWriter to do write files in "constant_memory" mode but it appears to do fail to write the temporary file when I have `None` as the value of optimization instead of "1" or "0".  In the `worksheet` class, it appears to sometimes, but not always check the **value** of the `optimization` attribute, rather than the **truthiness**.  So when I passed `None` by accident as the parameter, it wasn't treated consistently and I ended up with a error closing out the temporary file.



I am using Python version 3.4.3 and XlsxWriter 0.9.6 and Excel version n/a.

Here is some code that demonstrates the problem:

```python

import xlsxwriter

workbook = xlsxwriter.Workbook('hello.xlsx', {
    'constant_memory': None
})
worksheet = workbook.add_worksheet()

worksheet.write('A1', 'Hello world')

workbook.close()

```

results in:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "reproducer.py", line 10, in <module>
    workbook.close()
  File "/mnt/encrypted_data/cms_model/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/xlsxwriter/workbook.py", line 299, in close
    self._store_workbook()
  File "/mnt/encrypted_data/cms_model/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/xlsxwriter/workbook.py", line 607, in _store_workbook
    xml_files = packager._create_package()
  File "/mnt/encrypted_data/cms_model/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/xlsxwriter/packager.py", line 131, in _create_package
    self._write_worksheet_files()
  File "/mnt/encrypted_data/cms_model/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/xlsxwriter/packager.py", line 189, in _write_worksheet_files
    worksheet._assemble_xml_file()
  File "/mnt/encrypted_data/cms_model/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/xlsxwriter/worksheet.py", line 3434, in _assemble_xml_file
    self._write_optimized_sheet_data()
  File "/mnt/encrypted_data/cms_model/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/xlsxwriter/worksheet.py", line 4852, in _write_optimized_sheet_data
    self.row_data_fh.seek(0)
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'seek'
```

I think the correct fix is to update `worksheet.py ` and set all checks like:

```
if self.optimization == 1:
   ...
if self.optimization != 1:
   ...
```
to
```
if self.optimization:
   ...
if not self.optimization:
   ...
```

I'm prepared to do this and submit a pull request if you agree this is the correct solution.

应该是现在雪球组合ID的数字已经有7位的了，**extract_strategy_id**的**if len(strategy_url) != 8**判断失效

issue_title: Setting comma separated `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` values is not supported by FireFox.
issue_body:
Using version 2.1.2, initialising using:
```python
....
from flask.ext.cors import CORS
app = Flask(__name__)
CORS(app)
....
```

When making an AJAX request from a local machine on a non-standard port to an external server serving the above code running Flask 0.10.1 on Apache with mod_wsgi, the server responds with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: "http://localhost:8080, *"`. Such comma separated lists are not supported by FireFox, and gives the following error in the JavaScript debugging console: `Cross-Origin Request Blocked: The Same Origin Policy disallows reading the remote resource at http://hostna.me/app/route. (Reason: CORS header 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' does not match 'http://localhost:8080, *').`

When I ran `tests.py`, I've this strange error. It occurred from the first line of the `CoreTest.test_type` test case.

It seems that the ouput dict contains some integers which are integers of type `np.int64`. This numpy int type is not JSON serializable. Call `serialize` on your DataFrame from the `tests.py` file with the output_type="json"`parameter and do:

```python
import json
# 'df' from tests.py
json_data = serialize(df, render_to="chart", output_type="json")
# json.dumps(json_data) # raises the TypeError
type(json_data["series"][0]["data"][0][0])
# => numpy.int64
```

Unfortunately, when I read the code, the `serialize_series` function specifically, I cannot see where you can have such type.

keyID = (49,)

     (PyKCS11.CKA_CLASS, PyKCS11.CKO_PRIVATE_KEY),

])

mech = PyKCS11.Mechanism(PyKCS11.CKM_GOSTR3410, None)

   socket.send() raised exception

With a consumer, no exception and no error are raised.

I tried creating an async task that would simply try to open a new channel with a timeout. If the timeout exception is raised, I assume the connection is down then try to restart a new connection. The problem in this case is that I can't seem to stop the old consuming task. Calling the .close() method on the connection or channel blocks forever when the connection is down, as does a call to .cancel() on the consumer.

Do you have any thoughts on how a lost connection can be detected, and consumers rebooted with the new connection?

Thanks for all the work you are doing on this.

issue_title: PyGIWarning: GExiv2 was imported without specifying a version first.
issue_body:
Recent versions of PyGobject issue a warning if a library is used without specifying a version. This is to encourage application authors to specify versions and so allow multiple versions of some libraries to be installed.

The next release of Photini will specify the version of GExiv2, until then you can safely ignore this warning.

issue_title: pagination does not work on ajax data?
issue_body:
Hi, excuse me.  when I use ajax($http) to get data, the pagination does not work.
my code is here:  http://plnkr.co/edit/VEHXhIJketqcryvoia0V?p=preview
Will you give me a hand? I've try 'st-safe-src', but it is still the same.  thanks a lot.
best wishes.

Angular 1.6.4
Smart-table 2.1.9

Hi,

The `st-descending-first` attribute does not accept the value `false`. The descending first behavior is activated on the presence of the attribute and the value itself does not matter. Maybe this is by design, but it was unexpected.

In my case, ordering is dynamic as columns are generated from an array and some columns should descending first while other shouldn't. I tried to `st-descending-first="{{col.sort=='DESC'?true:false}}"` and the descending first was always applied.

If anyone has the same issue, there is a workaround using `ng-attr-st-descending-first="{{col.sort=='DESC'?true:undefined}}"`

Feel free to close if this is the expected behavior. Also worth noting that `st-descending-first` is not documented.

issue_title: Length of a escape sequence inside a string is taken into account
issue_body:
The length of a escape sequence is taken into account when calculating the col-width.
This is a problem when you want to display a colored string.

For example:

open = \033[1;32open\033[0m

-------------------+
open |
-------------------+

It would be great if there was a simple way to add an items per second widget.
Right now I'm using the TransferSpeed widgets, but at high speed they start to divide by 1024 instead of 1000 to add the SI prefix.
(and B/s could be changed to i/s)

issue_title: Expose request `proxy` kwd into the API to support both SOCKS & HTTP proxies
issue_body:
According to #123, http-proxy support is based on underlying `requests` module,
so it can be configured either with the `HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY` vars or with the `proxies` kwd in all requests methods.

As of April 2016 requests/requests#2953 [got merged](https://stackoverflow.com/a/36954255/548792) in `requests-2.10.1` and now SOCKS proxies are also supported.  I believe that the proper way now to specify both proxies is to use [the `proxies={}` method](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#socks), which supportes elaborate mappings, like that:

    proxies={
        'http': 'socks5://user:pass@host:port',
        'http://10.20.1.128': 'socks5h://user:pass@host:port'  # 'socks5h' for local dns-resolving.
    }
    requests.get('http://go.to', proxies=proxies)

With the current API of *exchangelib* this is impossible. 

1. Would it make sense to add a new kwd `proxies` immediately after the `verify` kwd?
2. Would it be better to support a generic `requests_kwds` dictionary instead?
   [edit:] For instance, [`cert` kwd](http://docs.python-requests.org/en/master/user/advanced/#ssl-cert-verification) might also bee used that way.

issue_title: Migration 0004 cannot create the unique constraint
issue_body:
After dealing with #535, I’m stuck at the migration 4. There are duplicate rows that contradict the new unique constraint. I get this error (sorry, it’s in French):

```
django.db.utils.IntegrityError: ERREUR:  n'a pas pu créer l'index unique « reversion_version_db_1aa3bc5d8084070f_uniq »
DETAIL:  La clé (db, content_type_id, object_id, revision_id)=(default, 31, 1054, 205095) est dupliquée.
```

I never did anything manual with django-reversion. I’ve been using it for 4 years on this project, and the only source of data creation in django-reversion tables is through the admin.
From what I see when I read the detail of the versions and the revision, the problem concerns an inline. An inline object was modified, but it seems the old and the new state of this inline object were linked to the revision of the main object. That’s why there is two times the same version for the same revision of the same object.

This is not an isolated case in the database, I removed hundreds of blocking cases like this, and the unique constraint still can’t be added.

I may have done something wrong without knowing it, but I can’t find it…

I use django-fancy-cache for the `only_get_keys` functionality.

In one of my views I watch for the `pdf` querystring and return the page as a PDF if it is present. `/foo/bar/` returns the normal HTML web page, `/foo/bar/?pdf` returns the page as a PDF.

I have set `only_get_keys=['pdf']` for this view. However, I noticed that in normal usage (where `/foo/bar/` is generally hit first) Django returns the HTML page when visiting `/foo/bar/?pdf`.

This is caused by the use of [cgi.parse_qs](https://docs.python.org/2/library/cgi.html#cgi.parse_qs), which will not return empty values by default.

```
>>> import cgi
>>> cgi.parse_qs('pdf')
{}
>>> cgi.parse_qs('pdf=')
{}
>>> cgi.parse_qs('pdf=a')
{'pdf': ['a']}
```

For my use case, I would like to pass `keep_blank_values=True` to the parser.

```
>>> import cgi
>>> cgi.parse_qs('pdf', keep_blank_values=True)
{'pdf': ['']}
```

It appears that these blank values are [valid according to the RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986#section-3.4).

For how django-fancy-cache uses the querystrings, I think it makes sense to use `keep_blank_values=True`. I would expect `/`, `/?pdf` and `/?pdf=foo` to all have different cache keys. However I admit that my use of `/?pdf` is sort of lazy, and I could just avoid this problem by watching for `?pdf=true` or something. I thought I would open an issue rather than submitting a pull request to see if there are any other opinions on the matter.

I'm happy to submit a pull request for this if people are ok with it.

When I try to extends the translations of an app with models already registered for translation, either I get an error or I get an incompletely patched model.

Lets say, for the sake of symplicity, that an existing ```my_app``` defines the following model:
```python
from django.db import models

class Entry(models.Model):
    title = models.CharField(max_length=200, blank=False)
    content = models.CharField(max_length=200, blank=False)
    description = models.CharField(max_length=200, blank=False)
```
and that the app creator provided the following translation.py:
```python
from modeltranslation.translator import translator, TranslationOptions
from my_app.models import Entry

class BrokenTranslation(TranslationOptions):
    fields = ('title', 'content')

translator.register(Entry, BrokenTranslation)
```

I’d like to extend it in my project without having to modify any file in the original app. So I made my own translation file like so:
```python
from modeltranslation.translator import translator
from my_app.translation import BrokenTranslation

class DesiredTranslation(BrokenTranslation):
    fields = ('description',)

translator.unregister(Entry)
translator.register(Entry, DesiredTranslation)
```
Unfortunately, django doesn't like it and any management command triggers:
```
django.core.management.base.SystemCheckError: SystemCheckError: System check identified some issues:

ERRORS:
my_app.Entry.content_en: (models.E006) The field 'content_en' clashes with the field 'content_en' from model 'my_app.entry'.
my_app.Entry.content_fr: (models.E006) The field 'content_fr' clashes with the field 'content_fr' from model 'my_app.entry'.
my_app.Entry.title_en: (models.E006) The field 'title_en' clashes with the field 'title_en' from model 'my_app.entry'.
my_app.Entry.title_fr: (models.E006) The field 'title_fr' clashes with the field 'title_fr' from model 'my_app.entry'.
```
This is because the Entry model is already patched and django refuses to add the translation fields again.
So, giving that the model is already patched, I came up with the following translation.py
```python
from modeltranslation.translator import translator, TranslationOptions

class DesiredTranslation(TranslationOptions):
    fields = ('description',)

translator.unregister(Entry)
translator.register(Entry, DesiredTranslation)
```
And django is happy. But I’m not:
```python
>>> from my_app.models import Entry
>>> from modeltranslation import manager
>>> manager.get_translatable_fields_for_model(Entry)
['description']
```
This leads to fields in the admin not being displayed properly (especially with the tabbed admin). Same for the templates.

Is there a way to add (or even remove) fields registered for translation for a given model? And shouldn’t the ```translator.unregister``` call remove the translation fields from the model?

issue_title: Language model incorrectly drops spaces for out-of-vocabulary words
issue_body:
Mozilla DeepSpeech will sometimes create long runs of text with no spaces:

```
omiokaarforfthelastquarterwastoget
```

This happens even with short audio clips (4 seconds) with a native American english speaker recorded using a high quality microphone in Mac OS X laptops. I've isolated the problem to interaction with the language model rather than the acoustic model or length of audio clips, as the problem goes away when the language model is turned off.

The problem might be related to encountering out-of-vocabulary terms.

I’ve put together test files with results that show the issue is related to the language model somehow rather than the length of the audio or the acoustic model.

I’ve provided 10 chunked WAV files at 16khz 16 bit depth, each 4 seconds long, that are a subset of a fuller 15 minute audio file (I have not provided that full 15 minute file, as a few shorter reproducible chunks are sufficient to reproduce the problem):

https://www.dropbox.com/sh/3qy65r6wo8ldtvi/AAAAVinsD_kcCi8Bs6l3zOWFa?dl=0

The audio segments deliberately include occasional out-of-vocabulary terms, mostly technical, such as “OKR”, “EdgeStore”, “CAPE”, etc.

Also in that folder are several text files that show the output with the standard language model being used, showing the garbled words together (`chunks_with_language_model.txt`):

```

so were trying again a maybeialstart this time

Running inference for chunk 2
omiokaarforfthelastquarterwastoget

Running inference for chunk 3
to car to state deloedmarchinstrumnalha

Running inference for chunk 4
a tonproductcaseregaugesomd produce sidnelfromthat

Running inference for chunk 5
i am a to do that you know 

Running inference for chunk 6
we finish the kepehandlerrwend finished backfileprocessing 

Running inference for chunk 7
and is he teckdatthatwewould need to do to split the cape 

Running inference for chunk 8
out from sir handler and i are on new 

Running inference for chunk 9
he is not monolithic am andthanducotingswrat 

Running inference for chunk 10
relizationutenpling paws on that until it its a product signal
```

Then, I’ve provided similar output with the language model turned off (`chunks_without_language_model.txt`):

```
Running inference for chunk 1
so we're tryng again ah maybe alstart this time

Running inference for chunk 2
omiokaar forf the last quarter was to get

Running inference for chunk 3
oto car to state deloed march in strumn alha

Running inference for chunk 4
um ton product  caser egauges somd produc sidnel from that

Running inference for chunk 5
am ah to do that ou nowith

Running inference for chunk 6
we finishd the kepe handlerr wend finished backfile processinga

Running inference for chunk 7
on es eteckdat that we would need to do to split the kae ha

Running inference for chunk 8
rout frome sir hanler and ik ar on newh

Running inference for chunk 9
ch las not monoliic am andthan ducotings wrat 

Running inference for chunk 10
relization u en pling a pas on that until it its a product signal
```

I’ve included both these files in the shared Dropbox folder link above.

Here’s what the correct transcript should be, manually done (`chunks_correct_manual_transcription.txt`):

```
So, we're trying again, maybe I'll start this time.

So my OKR for the last quarter was to get AutoOCR to a state that we could
launch an external alpha, and product could sort of gauge some product signal
from that. To do that we finished the CAPE handler, we finished backfill 
processing, we have some tech debt that we would need to do to split the CAPE 
handler out from the search handler and make our own new handler so its not
monolithic, and do some things around CAPE utilization. We are kind of putting
a pause on that until we get some product signal.
This shows the language model is the source of this problem; I’ve seen anecdotal reports from this message base and blog posts that this is a wide spread problem. Perhaps when the language model hits an unknown n-gram, it ends up combining all of them together rather than retaining the space between them.
```

This shows the language model is the source of this problem; I’ve seen anecdotal reports from the official message base and blog posts that this is a wide spread problem. Perhaps when the language model hits an unknown n-gram, it ends up combining all of them together rather than retaining the space between them.

Discussion around this bug started on the standard DeepSpeech discussion forum:
https://discourse.mozilla.org/t/text-produced-has-long-strings-of-words-with-no-spaces/24089/13
https://discourse.mozilla.org/t/longer-audio-files-with-deep-speech/22784/3

Same

- **GPU model and memory**:

Used CPU only version

- **Exact command to reproduce**:

I haven't provided my full modified `client.py` that segments longer audio, but to run with a language model using the standard `deepspeech` command against a known 4 seconds audio clip included in the Dropbox folder shared above you can run the following:

issue_title: Cacheing returns of __repr__ function of luigi.Task
issue_body:
luigi.Task.__repr__ generate task's string with it's parameters.
But if we build long dependency workflow which have many requirements having also many Parameters() that might be another task.
Sometimes building dependency on starting, 
It mainly caused by recursively generating string of parameters every time __repr__ called.
So I suggested that we caching return of __repr__ function using functools.lru_cache decorator like this:

```python
class Taks(object):
#...other fucntions.

    @lrcu_cache()
    def __repr__(self):
          # ... generating str of taks and return string

``` 

So this modification is simple but speeding up building dependency on starting.
I'd like you to consider this implementation.

Sometimes I want to make a task singleton, whatever its parameters are.

It is configratable to do that? Or is there any easy way to do that?

issue_title: Hang in s3.download_file with Celery worker in version 1.4.0
issue_body:
I've been using lots of boto3 calls in my Flask app for some time, but the switch to the latest boto3 v1.4.0 has broken my Celery workers. Something that may be unique about my app is that I use S3 to download a secure environment variables file before launching my app or workers. It appears that the new boto3 works with my app, but hangs when launching the Celery worker.

I would temporarily downgrade my boto3 to avoid the problem, but its been a long time since the last release, and I need the elbv2 support that only comes in 1.4.0.

I've created a tiny version of my worker (worker2.py) to demonstrate the problem. I've verified that using the previous version boto3 1.3.1 results in the worker launching properly. I see all prints and the Celery worker banner output.

If I install boto3 1.4.0, then the second print() statement "Download complete" is never reached.  Also note that I tried following the new doc example with boto3.resource and using s3.meta.client, but that fails as well.

``` python
#
# Stub Celery worker to demonstrate bug in Boto3 1.4.0. Works fine with previous version Boto3 1.3.1.
# Test with: celery worker -A worker2.celery
#
from flask import Flask
from celery import Celery
import boto3
import tempfile

celery = Celery(__name__, broker='amqp://guest:guest@localhost:5672//')

app = Flask(__name__)

s3 = boto3.client('s3', region_name='us-west-1')
env_file = 'APPNAME.APPSTAGE.env'
with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile() as s3_file:
    print("Downloading file...")
    response = s3.download_file('APPBUCKET', env_file, s3_file.name)
    print("Download complete!")
```

You can test it by running the following at the command line:

```
celery worker -A worker2.celery
```

Also note that just running the code downloads the file just fine with 1.4.0:

```
python worker2.py
```

I'm not sure whether this is a bug, a problem with the docs, or something I'm doing wrong, but I'm stuck and need guidance.

The code below corresponds to what's in the documentation [here](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/application-autoscaling.html#ApplicationAutoScaling.Client.put_scaling_policy):

```
def auto_scale_dynamodb(**kwargs):
    client = boto3.client('application-autoscaling')
    policy = kwargs['table_name'] + '-auto-scaling'
    resource = 'table/' + kwargs['table_name']

    response = client.put_scaling_policy(
        PolicyName=policy,
        ServiceNamespace='dynamodb',
        ResourceId=resource,
        ScalableDimension='dynamodb:table:ReadCapacityUnits',
        PolicyType='TargetTrackingScaling',
        TargetTrackingScalingPolicyConfiguration={
            'TargetValue' : 30000,
            'PredefinedMetricSpecification' : {
                'PredefinedMetricType' : 'DynamoDBReadCapacityUtilization'
            },
            'ScaleOutCooldown' : 30,
            'ScaleInCooldown' : 30
        }
    )
    return
```

But it throws this error: 
"Unknown parameter in input: "TargetTrackingScalingPolicyConfiguration", must be one of: PolicyName, ServiceNamespace, ResourceId, ScalableDimension, PolicyType, StepScalingPolicyConfiguration"

The [API docs](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/ApplicationAutoScaling/latest/APIReference/API_PutScalingPolicy.html) seem to agree with the boto3 docs, so I'm not sure what's up.

I've googled around, and found nothing. Suggestions?

Is it possible to read my configs (atleast S3 configs) from a s3cfg file ?
My usecase is to currently only use S3. This would make a lot of things simpler for me as I'd be able to use the same config for both s3cmd, boto3, etc.

issue_title: SSM get_parameter documentation missing fields in response object.
issue_body:
It looks like the docs at http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ are about 13 maintenance releases behind and there are new fields, specifically `ARN` and `LastModifiedDate`, returned by `boto3.client('ssm').get_parameter()` that are not documented.

We noticed it because we were using `json.dumps()` to log the response and our code started breaking after updating boto because the `datetime` isn't JSON-serializable.

Please bring the docs up to date with the current release, particularly since there are new fields in the return of at least one call.

I traced the changed method signature to https://github.com/python/cpython/commit/a5289bebbf3997554e2cf5f386abcdba2391469d with more details on the bug tracker http://bugs.python.org/issue12319.

I was able to get around the issue by hacking together the following patch.

```diff
diff --git a/botocore/awsrequest.py b/botocore/awsrequest.py
index a667bb5..828fe32 100644
--- a/botocore/awsrequest.py
+++ b/botocore/awsrequest.py
@@ -118,7 +118,7 @@ class AWSHTTPConnection(HTTPConnection):
             if line in (b'\r\n', b'\n', b''):
                 break

-    def _send_request(self, method, url, body, headers):
+    def _send_request(self, method, url, body, headers, *py36_extra):
         self._response_received = False
         if headers.get('Expect', b'') == b'100-continue':
             self._expect_header_set = True
@@ -126,7 +126,7 @@ class AWSHTTPConnection(HTTPConnection):
             self._expect_header_set = False
             self.response_class = self._original_response_cls
         rval = HTTPConnection._send_request(
-            self, method, url, body, headers)
+            self, method, url, body, headers, *py36_extra)
         self._expect_header_set = False
         return rval

@@ -143,7 +143,7 @@ class AWSHTTPConnection(HTTPConnection):
         msg = b"\r\n".join(bytes_buffer)
         return msg

-    def _send_output(self, message_body=None):
+    def _send_output(self, message_body=None, **py36_extra):
         self._buffer.extend((b"", b""))
         msg = self._convert_to_bytes(self._buffer)
         del self._buffer[:]
```

Happy to provide more details!

<!--- If describing a bug, tell us what happens instead of the expected behavior -->
Not implemented.

## Context
<!--- How has this issue affected you? What are you trying to accomplish? -->
<!--- Providing context helps us come up with a solution that is most useful in the real world -->
This is needed to harvest some work done in the context of EU grants and that is put on Zenodo (which mints Datacite DOIs). CC @annetteholtkamp.

## Screenshots (if appropriate):

The documentation should have an explicit chapter that is automatically generated after the JSONSchema.

Please feel free to suggest on best practice and how this should looke like.

The aim of this project is to allow anybody to discover which fields exist and how to use them, and their structure, without having to open the JSON.

Currently INSPIRE categories are stored at the same level of arXiv categories and other externally provided categories.

However, while cataloger are not supposed to touch externally provided categories, they are instead expected to curate INSPIRE categories. This poses INSPIRE categories in a privileged place.

It is proposed to move INSPIRE categories on a dedicated field, so that they are easier to edit (e.g. autocomplete can be enforced on the exact INSPIRE categories).

issue_title: Deploy issue connected to another network
issue_body:
I believe I found a bug when connected to non-FRC networking equipment. 

In \pyfrc\robotpy\installer.py, I'm having unpacking issues.

socket.getaddrinfo('roborio-4901-frc.local', None) returns
[(<AddressFamily.AF_INET6: 23>,  0,  0,  '',  ('fe80::280:2fff:fe17:3af1%8', 0, 0, 8)), (<AddressFamily.AF_INET: 2>, 0, 0, '', ('129.252.23.54', 0))]

However, socket.getaddrinfo('129.252.23.54', None) returns
[(<AddressFamily.AF_INET: 2>, 0, 0, '', ('129.252.23.54', 0))]

The first item in the list is invalid for the unpacking done by the 'for' loop on line 333.

Specifically the issue has to do with the last tuple returned. From the Python socket documentation: 
"sockaddr is a tuple describing a socket address, whose format depends on the returned family (a (address, port) **2-tuple for AF_INET**, a (address, port, flow info, scope id) **4-tuple for AF_INET6)**, and is meant to be passed to the socket.connect() method."

Here is my fix:
# pick the first address that is sock_stream
        for addr in addrs:
            if len(addr[4]) == 2: # Only addr with IPv4 addresses
                (_, t, _, _, (ip, _)) = addr
                if t == socket.SOCK_STREAM:
                    print("-> Found %s at %s" % (hostname, ip))
                    print()
                    hostname = ip
                    break

issue_title: PSF classes should have a "flux" attribute
[BUG]: output table in PSF Photometry classes
issue_body:
The indexing of the result table (the one with the fitted estimates) sometimes does not agree with the indexing of the table with the initial guesses (or the first estimates calculated by ``self.finder``).

This was caused by the ``nstar`` method which uses the ``group_by`` method from ``astropy.table.Table`` to group the entries in the initial guess table by their ``group_id`` which is likely to be different from the original order. 

Therefore, using hstack to combine those tables (e.g. [here](https://github.com/astropy/photutils/blob/master/photutils/psf/photometry.py#L280)) could lead to confusing results. For example, look at the lines 158 and 156 of the second table [here](https://github.com/astropy/photutils-datasets/blob/master/notebooks/ArtificialCrowdedFieldPSFPhotometry.ipynb).

I went ahead and fixed this in [70a04db](https://github.com/astropy/photutils/pull/471/commits/70a04db2faac755cb7a5c2421f5b42ce7ec598c1) by using the ``group_id`` indexing on both tables.

So, this can be closed once  #471 gets merged.

Originally on 2011-06-27

For general repository health monitoring purposes or also to re-factor certain computational intensive algorithm that are spread-around the Invenio codebase by pre-computing special collections it would be great to enhance `WebColl` and in general the `WebSearch` module to support a new type of collection called **System Collections**. These collections would be as normal collections in everything but their definition which can't be expressed by a normal query and must be therefore directly be specified in the code base. These System Collections are:
 Empty Records::
   containing all the records that have an ID but nothing else (i.e. no XM)
 Deleted Records::
   containing all the records that have **DELETED** in `980__%` (which is the convention in Invenio to marc a record as deleted.
 Restricted Records::
   containing all the records that belong to at least one restricted collection (this would greatly speed up the runtime computation for checking authorizations)
 Classified Records::
   containing all the records that belong to at least one real collection (if a record does not belong to such collection will surely not be available to anyone but its owner or superadmin)
 Unclassified Records::
   this is the counterpart of Classified Records, and will contain all the records that do not belong to at least a collection and are therefore accessible only to their owners or to superadmin
 Existing Records::
   this is the union of the Classified and Unclassified Records collections
 Public Records::
   this will be sort of an alias to the Home collection as it will contain all the records that are searchable from the home and are a priory discoverable by a crawler.
Note that a new record will initially not belong to any of the above collections (as webcoll will still need to be run). Subsequently, after webcoll will have classified it, it will either belong to the **Classified Records** collection or to the **Unclassified Records** collection

----

In order to make this collection safe, they will be actually called with an umprobable name such as "*System Collection -- Emtpy Records*" and be treated in special ways both by WebColl and by the WebSearch Admin Interface (e.g. it should not be possible to *delete* such a collection and if an admin attach these collections as real child of real collections, webcoll must ignore them in the computation of the real collection.)

----
`tabcreate.sql` can come with a default configuration where there is a non attached **System Collection** with all of the above collections attached as virtual collections.

issue_title: Better handling for unrecognized categorical levels?
issue_body:
There's a [request here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/34035912/patsy-new-levels-in-categorical-fields-in-test-data/45094458?noredirect=1#comment77176675_45094458) to add an option so that when an unrecognized categorical level is encountered, it should be encoded as all-zeros, which is apparently similar to what [scikit-learn's `DictVectorizer`](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.feature_extraction.DictVectorizer.html) does.

Technically this is something patsy could do. But AFAICT this would lead to terribly incorrect behavior in any kind of linear-ish model, and AFAIK linear-ish models are what one-hot encoding are for, so I don't understand what's going on here or why people want this, and I like to understand things before implementing them :-).

Specifically, the kind of issue I'm thinking about is... say you have a logistic regression model you're using to predict whether an apartment is occupied, with a model like `occupancy ~ C(city) + bedrooms + baths`. In this model, patsy will use treatment coding, so returning all-zeros for unrecognized cities is the same as predicting that they act just like whichever city was assigned as the reference category (probably the one that's first alphabetically). OK, but that's not what `DictVectorizer` does -- it always uses a full-rank encoding, so it's more like patsy's `occupancy ~ 0 + C(city) + bedrooms + baths`. Now in this model, the beta for each `city` gives something like the (logistic-transformed) mean occupancy for each city, and using all-zeros for unrecognized cities is equivalent to assuming that their mean occupancy is exactly 0 on the logistic scale, which is a *terrible* guess. You really want it to do something like... return a vector of `[1/n, 1/n, ..., 1/n]`, so that you're assuming unseen cities have similar occupancy to the average of the seen cities. Of course high-frequency cities and low-frequency cities are probably different too...

And other categorical encodings (e.g. polynomial coding) are even more of a mess.

So I'm not sure what to do here, if anything.

I feel like 99% of the time if you have an open category like this and don't want to use some principle solution like bayesian non-parametrics, then you instead want to do something like... keep the top 100 categories and bin everything else into "other", so then you actually have training data on the "other" category that's plausibly representative of what you'll see later (because in both cases it's relatively low frequency items). I guess this is also something patsy could potentially provide helpers for, though maybe it's more of a pandas thing.

CC: @ameuller

Is there a more direct way to expose the version number to the user?  Perhaps a `__version__` package attribute?  I dropped these two lines into the `/fire/__init__.py` file and it works fine for 2.7.6 and 3.6.4.

```python
import  pkg_resources
__version__ = pkg_resources.get_distribution('fire').version
```
It's not the most elegant solution.  [Other ideas.](https://packaging.python.org/guides/single-sourcing-package-version/#single-sourcing-the-version)

issue_title: Only plain literals should be parsed
issue_body:
@dbieber correctly pointed out in #95 that there is a more general problem with the parsing code. This is a follow up issue to that issue which only dealt with the most common case of a single argument.

Here are some cases demonstrating
```bash
$ python3 -c "import fire.parser as p; v = p.DefaultParseValue('[1+1]'); print(type(v), v)"
<class 'list'> [2]
$ python3 -c "import fire.parser as p; v = p.DefaultParseValue('{key: 1+1}'); print(type(v), v)"
<class 'dict'> {'key': 2} 
```

Some options are:
1. Accept the existing behaviour.
2. Always parse the whole argument as a string by doing `raise ValueError` in ``fire.parser._LiteralEval`
3. Parse only the sub expressions as strings

For 2. and 3. I think these would be the valid types from the `ast` module. 

 - `Str`
 - `Name` (converted to `Str`)
 - `Num`
 - `List`
 - `Tuple`
 - `Dict`
 - `Set`
 - `UnaryOp`

    581         y = y.astype(np.float64)
    582 
--> 583     check_consistent_length(X, y)
    584 
    585     return X, y

/usr/local/lib/python3.6/dist-packages/sklearn/utils/validation.py in check_consistent_length(*arrays)
    202     if len(uniques) > 1:

--> 204                          " samples: %r" % [int(l) for l in lengths])
    205 
    206 

ValueError: Found input variables with inconsistent numbers of samples: [127, 185]
```

here is a description of my series:

```
count    721.000000
mean       0.248312
std        6.259295
min       -0.245698
25%       -0.019635
50%        0.003867
75%        0.040906
max      168.075894
Name: 5m_return, dtype: float64
```

As I understand rdflib.Namespace and ClosedNamespace, they are both meant as auxiliaries to simplify the creation of URIRefs in Python code, e.g. `RDF.type` or `OWL.Class`. In addition, ClosedNamespace provides an additional vocabulary check, e.g., `RDF.spam` will raise an exception.

However, sometimes other uses of Namespace/ClosedNamespace can be useful, e.g., string comparison. At this stage, it is quite inconvenient that Namespace is derived from unicode, whereas ClosedNamespace is derived from the standard object, and the actual namespace string is an instance attribute (uri). In the interest of consistency, I propose that ClosedNamespace should also be derived from unicode (or even from Namespace).

Regards

issue_title: Default parameters break with collectionFormat csv
issue_body:
### Description

Can no longer define a `default` value for a query parameter with `type: array`, `items: {type: string}` and `collectionFormat: csv`. Possibly this is because of a change in swagger-spec-validator.

### Expected behaviour

In connexion version 1.3 (and swagger-spec-validator 2.1.0), a string default value worked in the above case.
An array default value would be fine too, and be more consistent with the `collectionFormat`.

### Actual behaviour

With a string default, eg. ```default: foobar```,
```
swagger_spec_validator.common.SwaggerValidationError:
("'foobar' is not of type 'array'\n\n
Failed validating 'type' in schema:\n
    {'collectionFormat': 'csv',\n
     'default': 'foobar',\n
     'in': 'query',\n
     'items': {'type': 'string'},\n
     'name': 'bar',\n
     'type': 'array'}\n\n
On instance:\n
    'foobar'", <ValidationError: "'foobar' is not of type 'array'">)
```
With an array default, eg. ```default: ["foobar"]```
```
  File ".../connexion/decorators/validation.py", line 55, in validate_type
    parts = value.split(",")
AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'split'
```

### Steps to reproduce

Use this `swagger.yaml` file: 
```
swagger: "2.0"
info:
  title: Thing
  version: "0"
paths:
  /foo:
    get:
      operationId: "test_app.api.foo"
      parameters:
        - $ref: "#/parameters/bar"
      responses:
        200:
          description: "Results."

parameters:
  bar:
    name: bar
    in: query
    type: array
    items:
      type: string
    collectionFormat: csv
    default: foobar
```
Use this `api.py`:
```
import connexion
def foo(bar):
  return bar
```
then execute:
```
import os
swagger_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__)), 'swagger.yaml')
app.add_api(swagger_path, base_path='/v0', strict_validation=True, validate_responses=False)
app.run()
```

### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
Python 3.6.5 :: Anaconda, Inc.
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
Version: 1.4.2

Also using swagger-spec-validator version 2.3.1.

### Description

After seeing #195 and taking the whole node left-pad debacle we should consider vendorizing at least some our dependencies like [pip](https://github.com/pypa/pip/tree/develop/pip/_vendor) and [requests](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/tree/master/requests/packages), among others, do.

### Pros

- We control the versions that we use, and are not dependent on what's is or is not in pypi;
- We are not vulnerable to packages getting removed or replaced on pypi.

### Cons

- We need to vendorize packages;
- We need to keep those packages updated.

### Description
When attempting to split a large swagger configuration file into smaller modular parts I am getting a ```SwaggerValidationError```

See:
* https://github.com/OAI/OpenAPI-Specification/blob/master/guidelines/REUSE.md
* https://apihandyman.io/writing-openapi-swagger-specification-tutorial-part-8-splitting-specification-file/

### Expected behaviour
Breaking swagger configurations into smaller files and referencing them should not produce an error

 ```
$ref: 'filename.yaml/#Reference'
```

### Actual behaviour
The references to other files raises this error:
 ``` 
swagger_spec_validator.common.SwaggerValidationError: unknown url type: 'filename.yaml/'
```


### Steps to reproduce
test.yaml
```yaml
swagger: "2.0"

info:
  title: "Test"
  version: "1.0"

basePath: /v1.0

produces:
  - application/json

consumes:
  - application/json

paths:
  /foo:
    get:
      operationId: test.foo
      responses:
        200:
          description: Foo
          schema:
            $ref: 'foo.yaml/#Foo'
```

foo.yaml
```yaml
Foo:
  description: Test foo
  type: object
  required:
    - foo
  properties:
    foo:
      type: string
      example: 'foo'
```

test.py:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import connexion
app = connexion.App(__name__, 9091)

def foo():
    return {'foo': 'bar'}

if __name__ == '__main__':
    app.add_api('test.yaml', validate_responses=True, strict_validation=True)
    app.run()
```


### Additional info:
Python 3.6.0
Conexion Version: 1.1.5
Is it compatible with windows? How to install on win 8?

https://github.com/systemd/python-systemd/blob/master/systemd/_reader.c

Reader_seek_realtime states (in docstring) that the argument _realtime_ is number of seconds (perhaps since the Unix epoch), but calls the _sd_journal_seek_realtime_usec_, that is, treats the argument as microseconds (usec).

In https://github.com/systemd/python-systemd/blob/master/systemd/journal.py, in seek_realtime, the argument is said to be "an integer unix timestamp" - which, as far as I know, means seconds (since the epoch). There is no mention of microseconds.

issue_title: Turn on test coverage reporting for CodeClimate
issue_body:
I'd like to make test coverage a more transparent metric by having it reported and displayed by CodeClimate. Tests on Manticore are run in parallel, which complicates reporting the overall coverage number a little bit.

The CodeClimate test reporter will need to get setup to run in 3 steps:

1. use `format-coverage` to format partial results
2. use `sum-coverage` to combine the partial results into a single file
3. use `upload-coverage` to send the results to CodeClimate

This means that after each batch of tests, we must run ($N is a unique identifier for the batch of tests):

```
./cc-test-reporter format-coverage --output "coverage/codeclimate.$N.json"
```

After all tests are finished, we must run ($PARTS is the number of payloads to sum):
```
cc-test-reporter sum-coverage --output - --parts $PARTS coverage/codeclimate.*.json | cc-test-reporter upload-coverage --input -
```

The [official docs](https://github.com/codeclimate/test-reporter#low-level-usage) talk about using S3 as a shared storage medium between parallel tests but I think this is unnecessary with our current Travis-CI and test setup. The partial test files should all persist until the end of the test run.

issue_title: Handling "Magnitude of gradient is bad: inf" in Python
issue_body:
A runtime error is raised [here](https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/dynet/training.cc#L69) if the gradient is `nan` or `inf` in `update()`.

Previous discussions of this issue: [here](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/dynet-users/ety7ZU3DNYc) and [here](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/dynet-users/RLCUI0073dk). In the latter, Matt suggests wrapping the call to `update()` in a `try ... catch`, but this will only work in C++ and not in Python, since it's not a Python exception being thrown.

I know it might be a problem with hyperparameters, but I would still like to be able to handle this in code (for example when doing hyperparameter search) rather than get thrown out of the program entirely.

I tried getting the gradient and checking it myself:
```
...
loss.backward()
if loss.gradient() not in (np.inf, np.nan):
        trainer.update()
...
```
but this gives me the following error:
`RuntimeError: Requested gradient for node 169282, but backward pass was computed from node 169282`
coming from [here](https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/0e4550542825dc7e9ceee1158490f531c49f3e0b/dynet/exec.cc#L42).

Is this a bug? Shouldn't it be possible to get the gradient of a node up to which the backward pass was computed?

It would be really nice to be able to install DyNet through pip. Currently we are working on removing the dependency on Boost, which will make this easier, and are almost done: https://github.com/clab/dynet/issues/84

If there is anyone in the community who is familiar with setting up the pip build system, @xunzhang and I would greatly appreciate your help in getting DyNet set up with it.

Would it be possible to the user to change the default py-art name ('reflectivity' etc.) while **installing**. I know all functions have options to overwrite the standard, but we have here in SIMEPAR a really (internaly)-uniform standard, but as it is different than py-art I must always correct it. ARTview is also having some difficulties because of that.

issue_title: BigQuery: Failed to Parse JSON: Closing quote expected in string (non-ASCII characters)
issue_labels: [{'id': 82126549, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20bigquery', 'name': 'api: bigquery', 'color': 'f8f8f8', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604243, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p2', 'name': 'priority: p2', 'color': 'fef2c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438580, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'd93f0b', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Hello, 

I'd like to ask for some help on an issue I'm having.

Here's a sample code I created just to illustrate the problem:

```
import google.cloud.bigquery as bq
from google.cloud.bigquery import LoadJobConfig

con = bq.Client.from_service_account_json('/key.json')

from io import StringIO
data = "{'field1': '3 meses'}\n"
table = con.dataset('dataset_name').table('table_name')

conf = LoadJobConfig()
conf.write_disposition = 'WRITE_TRUNCATE'
conf.autodetect = True
conf.source_format = 'NEWLINE_DELIMITED_JSON'

r = con.load_table_from_file(StringIO(data), table, job_config=conf).result()
print(r.done())
print(r.errors)
```

If I run this code it works normally. But when a non-ASCII character is present in the string, such as:

    data = "{'field1': '3 mêses'}\n"

Then I get the error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 20, in <module>
    r = con.load_table_from_file(StringIO(data), table, job_config=conf).result()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/cloud/bigquery/job.py", line 528, in result
    return super(_AsyncJob, self).result(timeout=timeout)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/google/api_core/future/polling.py", line 111, in result
    raise self._exception
google.api_core.exceptions.BadRequest: 400 Error while reading data, error message: Failed to parse JSON: Closing quote expected in string; Could not parse value

```

I confirmed that `bq load` command works normally, it's just the Google Cloud API that I got this error. Am I missing something?

The only difference I could see from `bq load` command to the API is the mimetype is `application/octet-stream` in bq and `application/json` in API (I don't even know if this makes any difference, it's just the only difference I could find so far).

I'm using Python3.6 running from the latest public docker image with no virtualenvs. If you need anything else please let me know,

Thanks,
https://readthedocs.org/projects/google-cloud-python/builds/4436621/

issue_title: Feature Request: Use sphinx autosummary in API reference docs
issue_body:
I like how [Pandas](https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/blob/master/doc/source/api.rst) and [Pandas-GBQ](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pydata/pandas-gbq/master/docs/source/api.rst) use [Sphinx autosummary](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/ext/autosummary.html) to show a browsable [table](https://pandas-gbq.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html) or [tables](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/api.html) above the detailed documentation.

We've gotten feedback that the [BigQuery client API reference docs](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/bigquery/reference.html) are hard to browse. I think an autosummary table (or a few task-oriented autosummary tables) would really help with this.

issue_body:
Currently the logging library is writing files to a magical path:  `/var/log/app_engine/`.  This is not an official supported path, and would only work for App Engine.  At any point -that path could stop working. 

The only officially supported ways to write logs are to write to stdout or stderr, or to use the cloud logging APIs.  Come chat with me if you have questions!

[https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/logging/google/cloud/logging/handlers/app_engine.py#L34][1]

issue_body:

   General, Core, and Other are also allowed as types

2. OS type and version

Some example errors that I see on stackdriver, but not correlated with the time of OOM

```

grpc._channel._Rendezvous: <_Rendezvous of RPC that terminated with (StatusCode.ABORTED, The operation was aborted.)>

```
Also, see code example below

6. Steps to reproduce

* Create  a kubernetes pod with the code example
* Fill a PubSub topic/subscription with a couple of million of messages
* Run the pod to consume the messages
* Watch memory usage keep climbing until OOM occurs and pod gets killed/restarted by Kubernetes
* This seems to occur even if I set a flow_control on messages

7. Code example

The code that I am using is basically a copy of this documentation: https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/pubsub/#subscribing

```bash
import os
from google.cloud import pubsub
import time
import datetime

subscriber = pubsub.SubscriberClient()
topic = 'projects/{project_id}/topics/{topic}'.format(
    project_id='<redacted>',
    topic='<redacted>',  # Set this to something appropriate.
)
subscription_name = 'projects/{project_id}/subscriptions/{sub}'.format(
    project_id='<redacted>',
    sub='<redacted>',  # Set this to something appropriate.
)

try:
    subscription = subscriber.create_subscription(subscription_name, topic)
except:
    # If subscription exists
    subscription_path = subscriber.subscription_path('<redacted>', '<redacted>')
    subscription = subscriber.subscribe(subscription_path)

def callback(message):
    print("""{} Status: subscription._load: {}, subscription.flow_control.max_bytes {}, subscription.flow_control.resume_threshold: {},subscription._consumer.paused: {},len(subscription.managed_ack_ids): {}""".
        format(datetime.datetime.utcnow().replace(tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
            subscription._load,
            subscription.flow_control.max_bytes,
            subscription.flow_control.resume_threshold, 
            subscription._consumer.paused,
            len(subscription.managed_ack_ids)))
    print(message.data)
    message.ack()

future = subscription.open(callback)
future.result()
```

Example print outs I am seeing:
```
2018-01-26T16:58:51.810704+00:00 Status: subscription._load: 1.1396981029742239e-07, subscription.flow_control.max_bytes 10976591052.800001, subscription.flow_control.resume_threshold: 0.8,subscription._consumer.paused: False,len(subscription.managed_ack_ids): 9
```


Kubernetes pod is QoS guaranteed with the following limits and requests
```
        resources:
          limits:
            memory: "4500Mi"
            cpu: "2.5"
          requests:
            memory: "4500Mi"
            cpu: "2.5"
```

My kubernetes pods run on a cluster made up of n1-highmem-8 (8 vCPUs, 52 GB memory) machines.

I think the issue might be that psutil.virtual_memory() doesn't seem to give the correct memory amount. It's giving the node's memory limits, but not the Kubernetes pod's limits? https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/master/pubsub/google/cloud/pubsub_v1/types.py#L58
```
FlowControl.__new__.__defaults__ = (
    psutil.virtual_memory().total * 0.2,  # max_bytes: 20% of total RAM
    float('inf'),                         # max_messages: no limit
    0.8,                                  # resume_threshold: 80%
)
```

In the [google_compute_engine/accounts/accounts_utils.py](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages/blob/e843cc6919bbed8203ef8ce87b5a3120d4e86800/google_compute_engine/accounts/accounts_utils.py#L173-L174) file on lines 173-174. It sets insecure permissions of 755 on home directories which is failing our audit requirement for the [RHEL7 STIG](http://rhel7stig.readthedocs.io/en/latest/medium.html#rhel-07-020700-all-files-and-directories-contained-in-local-interactive-user-home-directories-must-have-mode-0750-or-less-permissive). Is the 755 permission required? Or can I make a PR setting it to something safer that will pass audits like 750?
Support for `fox` should be added. One issue is that the tests are different for each version of python, and currently need to be aware of which version of python is being executed.

issue_title: PythonInterperter should support creating bare interpreters.
issue_body:
The only remaining public APIs (the constructor is fapp not public) all route through `from_binary` which creates a `PythonInterpreter` with all the extras installed in that interpreter's `site_packages`, etc. As such, creating a bare interpreter requires something like:
```python
interpreter_with_extras = PythonInterpreter.from_binary(binary_path)
bare_interpreter = PythonInterpreter(binary_path, interpreter_with_extras.identity, extras=None)
```

It's perhaps enough to plumb an `include_site_extras=True` parameter through `from_binary` that, when passed as false, could produce a bare interpreter.

issue_title: go_fetch includes all packages in repo
issue_body:
go_fetch fetches more code than we need because it looks at all packages in a go_remote_lib, not just the packages that are listed as dependencies.

go_fetch will fetch a go_remote_library/go_remote_libraries's source and then search for dependencies transitively. Pants searches for the dependencies of all go packages in the repo so it can compile each (though it omits test dependencies).

Many go projects include different packages with different dependencies. For instance, github.com/spf13/cobra/doc includes github.com/cpuguy83/go-md2man/md2man . We do not use github.com/spf13/cobra/doc (though we do use github.com/spf13/cobra , without the doc).

We shouldn't have to think about (or have BUILD files for) libraries we never use solely because they are dependencies of other packages in libraries that we use some packages in.

issue_title: allow lambdas in @rules to perform Get requests somehow
issue_body:
Example from #5815: we would like to write this code, using an API used elsewhere in the PR for platform-specific switching. Currently, `platform.resolve_platform_specific()` accepts a dict of `os_name -> 0-argument function`, and would be invoked as follows (excerpted from the body of an `@rule`):
```python
linker = platform.resolve_platform_specific({
  'darwin': lambda: Get(Linker, XCodeCLITools, native_toolchain._xcode_cli_tools),
  'linux': lambda: Get(Linker, Binutils, native_toolchain._binutils),
})
```

Get requests need to be in the body of an `@rule`, because we parse them out of the function's ast in `src/python/pants/engine/rules.py`. Get requests within a lambda, as above, will "compile" correctly, but the scheduler execution will fail, because the types in the Get requests aren't registered (and there's also no `yield` allowed in lambdas).

Because Get requests can only be made in the body of an `@rule`, you cannot use them in other functions. It would make sense to allow lambdas, otherwise we have no way to abstract out conditional logic that may result in Get requests to another function (all of our Get requests must be expressions evaluated in the `@rule` function scope). With lambdas allowed, the Get requests are still in the body of the `@rule`, so I don't think this makes things too magical. I think this may be possible by expanding the ast parsing in the `rule` definition.

There may be another interface besides lambdas that I'm not considering.

issue_title: serialize_compact() on python3-branch returns bytes, which is considered unserializable
issue_body:
Python3's own json-library refuses to serialize the result of serialize_compact() since it is not a py3 str but a py3 bytes. This breaks our code, at the least. I'm not sure about the best way to solve it. A flag that sets encoding and if set spits out str and not bytes? Doing another walkthrough throught the code and choosing when to use str vs. bytes instead of always using bytes? Add to the json-library upstream so that it can serialize bytes, assuming that you tell it the encoding to use?

Anyway, the documentation for the function says it returns str, which it does not do on python3.

issue_title: images.build failing on container build output
issue_body:
The ImageCollection.build() method parses the `docker build` output to extract the name of the image being built by looking for the string "Successfully built <hash>".  Some commands in a Dockerfile can also produce this string causing the built() method to return early with an incorrect image name.

Example output from a `RUN pip install cffi` command:
```
 ...
Building wheels for collected packages: cffi, pycparser
  Running setup.py bdist_wheel for cffi
  Stored in directory: /root/.cache/pip/wheels/ea/df/5c/7d11cd4eec7b94be3b0ec4ed0076e0f38846a22c7200576149
  Running setup.py bdist_wheel for pycparser
  Stored in directory: /root/.cache/pip/wheels/bc/07/7e/cd81c0b23417ebaed9eb2584c00bd539871d5309a7a2324953
Successfully built cffi pycparser
...
```

A partial fix is to make the regex used to search for the string more restrictive by adding start and end anchors.  This can still result in some false positives.
A more complete fix only checks the last line of output for the "Success..." string.
PR in progress.

Docker 1.5 introduced the ability to ignore both the .dockerignore and the Dockerfile (in the .dockerignore file), but this is processed on the server since and only for dealing with the build cache (ie, when someone does ADD., Dockerfile changes can be ignored).

If Dockerfile is in .dockerignore, Dockerfile won't be sent to the server, so the server can't build it

Basically, .dockerignore, and Dockerfile should be treated as special case entries in the .dockerignore so that they still get sent up to the server.

issue_title: dfu_transport_ble.py  could re-connect to incorrect BLE-device.
issue_body:
When doing a DFU of a combined SD + APP image, after the SD update, nrfutil should re-connect to the bootloader to do the APP update. However, I've experienced cases where nrfutil re-connects to other BLE-devices nearby, and the DFU fails.
I've debugged and found the cause of this issue.

## When doing the SD update:
In the class DFUAdapter, when connect() is called, the parameters "target_device_name" and "target_device_addr" are given and stored in the class instance.
Upon receiving advertisement reports, the target_device values are compared to the BLE address and BLE device name of the advertising device. If one of them match nrfutil will initiate a connection.

However, DFUAdapter keeps getting advertisement reports until the connection is successfully completed. In some occasions it is possible to get advertisement packets without a BLE device name.
Here is a short log from a connection:

BLE: connect: target address: 0xNone, target name: DfuTest
BLE: Scanning...
Received advertisement report, address: 0xDCF79209A18C, device_name: DfuTest
BLE: Found target advertiser, address: 0xDCF79209A18C, name: DfuTest
BLE: Connecting to 0xDCF79209A18C
Received advertisement report, address: 0xDCF79209A18C, device_name: 
BLE: Found target advertiser, address: 0xDCF79209A18C, name: 
BLE: Connecting to 0xDCF79209A18C
BLE: Connected to <pc_ble_driver_py.ble_driver.BLEGapAddr object at 0x059929B0>
BLE: Connected to target
BLE: Service Discovery

From these lines of log and by looking at the code, we can now see that the value of self.target_device_name is an empty string.

## When doing the following APP update:
The empty string stored for self.target_device_name will be an issue when we try to re-connect to the bootloader after the SD update is succeeded.
This is because nrfutil will connect to a device with an empty BLE device name (empty string).


# FIX
In the on_gap_evt_adv_report() in DFUAdapter():
Remove the line:  self.target_device_name = dev_name

```python
ack_task = nidaqmx.Task()
